mappings.rationale: Directly covers Central African history, including the emergence of Bantu-speaking peoples and the Kongo, Luba, and Lunda kingdoms.
The section explicitly references archaeological sources and historiographic problems in understanding Central African Stone Age cultures.
The ethnography of Central Africa, the Bantu-speaking peoples, and the Mongo people involve anthropological methods and frameworks.
The development of the Kongo kingdom and the Luba and Lunda kingdoms from c. 1400 represent indigenous empire-building in Central Africa.
The origins of Central African cultures in the Stone Age and the emergence of Bantu-speaking peoples connect to broader questions of prehistoric human development.
The religious and cultural practices of Central African peoples, including the Kongo and Luba kingdoms, are part of the broader African religious landscape.
The political structures of the Bantu states, particularly the Kongo, Luba, and Lunda kingdoms, are foundational to understanding Central African political development.
